Carboxysome shell vertex protein CcmL — UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot Q8DKB4 — is a 99-residue protein from Thermosynechococcus vestitus (strain NIES-2133 / IAM M-273 / BP-1) (NCBI taxon 197221); gene ccmL.
Function: Probably forms vertices in the carboxysome, a polyhedral inclusion where RuBisCO (ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase, rbcL-rbcS) is sequestered. Has been modeled to induce curvature upon insertion into an otherwise flat hexagonal molecular layer of CcmK subunits
Subcellular location: Carboxysome. Note=This cyanobacterium makes beta- type carboxysomes (Probable). Probably forms vertices in the carboxysome (Probable)
GO annotations: carboxysome (component); structural constituent of carboxysome shell (function); carbon fixation (process); photosynthesis (process)
Sequence features: 8 strand; 2 helix; 1 chain; 1 domain [1-83 (BMV)]; 1 turn

<protein></protein>

Protein translocase subunit SecA 1 — UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot P0A5Y9 — is a 949-residue protein from Mycobacterium bovis (strain ATCC BAA-935 / AF2122/97) (NCBI taxon 233413); gene secA1.
Function: Part of the Sec protein translocase complex. Interacts with the SecYEG preprotein conducting channel. Has a central role in coupling the hydrolysis of ATP to the transfer of proteins into and across the cell membrane, serving as an ATP-driven molecular motor driving the stepwise translocation of polypeptide chains across the membrane
Catalytic activity: Reaction=ATP + H2O + cellular proteinSide 1 = ADP + phosphate + cellular proteinSide 2.; EC=7.4.2.8; Evidence=;

====================================================================================================
>sp:Q9IMP4 RNA-directed RNA polymerase catalytic subunit [Influenza C virus (strain C/Johannesburg/1/1966)]
<protein></protein>

RNA-directed RNA polymerase catalytic subunit — UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot Q9IMP4 — is a 754-residue protein from Influenza C virus (strain C/Johannesburg/1/1966) (NCBI taxon 100673); gene PB1.
Function: RNA-dependent RNA polymerase which is responsible for replication and transcription of virus RNA segments. The transcription of viral mRNAs occurs by a unique mechanism called cap-snatching. 5' methylated caps of cellular mRNAs are cleaved after 10-13 nucleotides by PA. In turn, these short capped RNAs are used as primers by PB1 for transcription of viral mRNAs. During virus replication, PB1 initiates RNA synthesis and copy vRNA into complementary RNA (cRNA) which in turn serves as a template for the production of more vRNAs
Catalytic activity: Reaction=RNA(n) + a ribonucleoside 5'-triphosphate = RNA(n+1) + diphosphate; Xref=Rhea:RHEA:21248, Rhea:RHEA-COMP:14527, Rhea:RHEA- COMP:17342, ChEBI:CHEBI:33019, ChEBI:CHEBI:61557, ChEBI:CHEBI:140395; EC=2.7.7.48; Evidence=;
